SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s main empirical claims are clearly stated and supported by systematic LSTM language-model experiments across multiple languages, with appropriate baselines (word, char bi-LSTM/CNN, Morfessor, BPE) and reported hyperparameters. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the methodology uses sensible metrics (perplexity, nearest neighbors) and hand-annotated morphological analyses for targeted comparisons. Minor concerns include the fixed 5,000-word output vocabulary limiting open-vocabulary conclusions and lack of mention of released code for full reproducibility, but these do not undermine the primary experimental findings.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper examines morphology in the context of neural language modeling, a central topic for top-tier NLP conferences; its comparisons of character/char-trigram/morph/BPE representations and composition functions directly address core representation questions in the field. The methodology (systematic LSTM language-model experiments across multiple languages and typologies, standard perplexity evaluation, and comparisons to hand-annotated morphological analyses and common baselines) aligns with community expectations. The datasets and baselines cited (UD, Ling et al. 2015, Morfessor, BPE, character CNN/LSTM baselines) show the work is positioned within the relevant research conversation and targets the right audience.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important problem—how subword representations (characters, char trigrams, morphs, BPE) capture morphology for language modeling—using systematic multilingual experiments and established baselines (char LSTM/CNN, Morfessor, BPE). Its finding that char-level models and unsupervised segmentation fall short of models with gold morphological analyses is likely to change researchers' expectations about the sufficiency of character-only approaches and motivate work on better unsupervised morphology or annotated resources. By evaluating composition functions and multiple languages, it opens substantive follow-up research directions and is likely to be built upon by both LM and morphology communities, with implications beyond this specific modeling setup.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper performs a systematic, cross-lingual empirical comparison of character, character‑trigram, unsupervised morphs (Morfessor/BPE), and manual morphological analyses within LSTM language models—an evaluation-style contribution that meaningfully extends prior character-aware LM work (e.g., Ling et al., Kim et al., and subword/Enriching-word-vector lines). However, the modeling components (char/CNN/bi-LSTM composition, BPE, Morfessor) are existing methods, so the novelty lies primarily in the comprehensive experimental protocol and the negative finding that unsupervised segmentation and pure character models do not match manual morphological analyses. This yields a moderate (incremental but useful) advance rather than a fundamentally new method or theory.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper runs a broad, systematic set of experiments comparing characters, character trigrams, Morfessor, BPE and manual morphological analyses across multiple languages and composition functions (add, bi-LSTM, CNN), and reports hyperparameters and quantitative (perplexity) plus qualitative analyses—supporting the main claims. Appropriate ablations / comparisons (composition functions, segmentation methods, baselines like word-level LSTM and character/CNN models from prior work such as Ling et al. and Kim et al.) are present, though open-vocabulary prediction and code release are not addressed which are minor reproducibility gaps. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and the paper explicitly discusses negative results and limitations (fixed output vocabulary, unsupervised segmentation as an imperfect proxy), so overall the experimental depth is strong with only modest missing elements.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against a comprehensive set of relevant and recent baselines (word-level LSTM, character bi-LSTM from Ling et al. (2015), CharCNN (Kim et al., 2016), Morfessor, BPE) and also includes models using gold/manual morphological analyses from UD. Comparisons appear fair and controlled—same LSTM language model, shared evaluation metric (perplexity), and multilingual datasets—while limitations (fixed output vocabulary) are acknowledged. The authors explicitly report when prior or alternative methods perform comparably or better (e.g., manual morphological analyses outperform character/unsupervised methods) and provide systematic analyses (quantitative perplexity and qualitative nearest-neighbor) of differences. Related work coverage is described as thorough and includes the most relevant competing approaches.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized and presents a logical flow from motivation through method to experiments and conclusions, with the main contributions clearly stated early (claims about character representations, bi-LSTM trigrams, and limits of unsupervised segmentation). Notation and experimental details appear consistent and well documented (hyperparameters reported), and the experimental setup is systematic across languages and baselines. Figures/tables and qualitative examples (nearest-neighbor analyses) support the claims and the related work coverage is thorough; minor presentation shortcomings (e.g., limited discussion of code availability and the fixed-output-vocabulary limitation) keep it from an exceptional rating.

main_claims: Character representations are effective across many languages for language modeling.
A combination of character trigram representations composed with bi-LSTMs outperforms most other settings tested.
Character-level models do not match the predictive accuracy of models with access to explicit (hand-annotated) morphological analyses.
Unsupervised morphological segmentation methods (e.g., Morfessor, BPE) are inadequate substitutes for manual morphological analysis in terms of predictive accuracy.
_summary.methodology: Systematic experiments using an LSTM language model where word representations are computed by composing subword units (characters, character trigrams, morphs from Morfessor or BPE) via different composition functions (addition, bi-LSTM, CNN), comparing models on language modeling perplexity and qualitative nearest-neighbor analyses across languages of different morphological typologies.
datasets: Datasets from Ling et al. (2015) for English and Turkish
UD (Universal Dependencies) datasets (used for hand-annotated morphological analyses for Czech and Russian)
metrics: Cosine similarity for nearest-neighbor qualitative analysis
limitations: Character-level and unsupervised segmentation models do not reach the predictive accuracy of models using manual morphological analyses.
The language model predicts only from a fixed output vocabulary (most frequent 5,000 words plus UNK), so open-vocabulary prediction was not addressed.
Unsupervised morphological segmentation is only an approximate proxy for true morphological analysis.
